title: Pixel Density Guide
summary: Understand pixels-per-foot, camera resolution, field of view, target width, and whether a view has enough detail for the task.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates target pixel density and compares it to the detail goal so you can see whether the camera view is wide, detailed, or overloaded.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it after rough field-of-view planning when you need to know whether the scene has enough image detail for detection, observation, recognition, or identification-style review.
bullets: Early design planning.
Camera placement review.
Expansion or retrofit validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical physical security planning terms.

items.label: Horizontal Resolution (px)
items.description: The number of horizontal pixels available in the camera image.
items.whyItMatters: More horizontal pixels can improve detail, but only if the lens and scene width place those pixels on the target.
items.commonMistake: Assuming 4K always solves detail problems without checking the actual scene width.

items.label: Horizontal FOV (deg)
items.description: The horizontal viewing angle used by the camera/lens.
items.whyItMatters: FOV determines how widely the camera spreads its available pixels. Wider FOV usually lowers pixels per foot.
items.commonMistake: Leaving a camera in a wide overview shot while expecting recognition-quality detail.

links.label: Use FOV width as the upstream geometry
links.note: Field of View helps confirm the coverage width that pixel density depends on.

items.label: Distance (ft)
items.description: The distance from the camera to the target plane where detail is required.
items.whyItMatters: Pixel density falls as the target plane gets farther away for the same camera angle and resolution.
items.commonMistake: Testing close to the camera and assuming the same detail will hold at the far side of the scene.

items.label: Target Pixel Density (PPF)
items.description: The desired pixels-per-foot threshold for the target or task.
items.whyItMatters: The target threshold defines what success means. A camera can cover the area but still fail the detail goal.
items.commonMistake: Using an arbitrary PPF number without matching it to the actual operational requirement.

items.label: Target Width (ft)
items.description: The real-world width of the object or zone used for detail evaluation.
items.whyItMatters: Target width helps translate image pixels into practical detail on the object that matters.
items.commonMistake: Using total scene width when the actual target is much smaller.

links.label: Check plate-specific detail separately
links.note: Use the plate range tool when the target is a license plate rather than a general scene.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the calculated detail appears to meet the target with usable margin. Watch means the view may work but should be validated against the real camera, compression, lighting, and mounting. Risk means the scene is likely too wide, too far, or too low-resolution for the intended detail.
bullets: Pixel density is a detail check, not a complete camera design by itself.
A higher PPF value usually requires a narrower view, higher resolution, closer placement, or multiple cameras.
Always validate detail with actual image samples when recognition or evidence quality matters.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most physical security planning problems come from treating camera coverage, recognition detail, mounting position, and lighting as separate decisions. In the field, those choices interact: a wider view lowers pixel density, a higher mount changes target geometry, and poor lighting can erase detail that looked acceptable on paper.
bullets: Sizing a camera view only by how wide the scene looks, without checking target detail.
Using clean daytime assumptions for a scene that must also work at night, in glare, or under motion.
Ignoring overlap, obstructions, mounting limits, and the actual target plane.
Treating calculated values as final design approval instead of documented planning assumptions.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: These guides explain the planning logic behind the tool. They do not replace manufacturer documentation, site survey, camera testing, client acceptance criteria, code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Validate against the actual camera model, lens, sensor, encoder settings, and mounting location.
Confirm lighting, reflections, obstructions, weather, and target movement before relying on a design.
Use calculated outputs as design evidence and review notes, not as a substitute for field validation.
Leave practical margin when the scene is important, difficult, or expected to change.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, on-site testing, legal review, privacy review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Document assumptions before implementation.
Review the design with the owner, integrator, manufacturer, and applicable authority when required.
Test representative views before treating a camera placement as final.
